passageOrStimulus: A bakery says that extending its opening hours increased daily sales, because revenue rose in the month after the change.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

choices.text: A large nearby bakery closed during that same month, diverting customers to the bakery in question.
The bakery introduced a new logo.
Customers reported liking the smell of the bread.
The bakery hired one extra cashier.
The town held a food festival that month.

distractorRationale.B: A new logo is a cosmetic change with no shown effect on sales, so it neither explains the revenue rise nor undercuts the hours claim; the weakener must supply an alternative cause, as the nearby bakery's closure does.
distractorRationale.C: Liking the smell is a vague positive detail that does not account for the timing of the increase; only an alternative cause for that month's revenue, like a competitor closing, weakens the argument.
distractorRationale.D: One extra cashier speeds service but does not provide an independent reason sales rose that month; the real weakener offers a competing cause, namely the rival bakery's closure diverting customers.
distractorRationale.E: A food festival could plausibly raise sales, but the stem points to the single best weakener; the competitor's closure directly redirects that bakery's own customers, making it the cleaner alternative cause. Treat this as a tempting near-miss and pick the most direct rival explanation.

explanation: A nearby competitor closed at the same time, which can explain the sales increase without any connection to the extended hours.

passageOrStimulus: A software team claims that replacing weekly meetings with a shared dashboard will save time without reducing coordination.
stem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the claim depends?

choices.text: Team members will actually check the dashboard regularly and update it accurately.
The dashboard will use bright colors.
Weekly meetings are always boring.
The team works in the same office.
Management prefers dashboards to meetings.

distractorRationale.B: Bright colors are an irrelevant design detail; the claim's success hinges on the dashboard being checked and updated, which is what choice A supplies.
distractorRationale.C: Whether meetings are boring is a value judgment unrelated to whether the dashboard saves time without losing coordination; the needed assumption is that people will actually use it accurately.
distractorRationale.D: Being in the same office is not required for the dashboard plan to work; the argument instead depends on members consulting and updating the dashboard reliably.
distractorRationale.E: Management's preference does not bear on whether coordination is preserved; the load-bearing assumption is that the dashboard will be checked and kept accurate.

explanation: The argument assumes that the dashboard will actually be consulted and reliably updated.

passageOrStimulus: A clinic introduced a reminder text system and says it reduced missed appointments.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim?

choices.text: The same patients had a lower no-show rate after the texts were introduced than before, even after adjusting for season.
Some patients preferred phone calls.
The clinic also painted the waiting room.
Reminder texts cost less than paper notices.
The clinic saw more social-media followers.

distractorRationale.B: A preference for phone calls says nothing about whether texts cut no-shows and if anything mildly undercuts the claim; the strengthener is a measured drop among the same patients.
distractorRationale.C: Painting the waiting room introduces a confound rather than support, since it offers a rival cause for any change; the real strengthener isolates the texts by tracking the same patients.
distractorRationale.D: Lower cost addresses budget, not effectiveness at reducing absences; to strengthen the causal claim you need the season-adjusted decline in no-shows.
distractorRationale.E: More social-media followers is unrelated to appointment attendance; the link is reinforced only by the same patients showing fewer no-shows after the texts.

explanation: A measured decrease in absences among the same patients directly reinforces the causal link.

passageOrStimulus: A survey found that among people who bought reusable water bottles last year, most also reported carrying a bottle on at least four days per week this year.
stem: Which of the following can be inferred?

choices.text: Many recent buyers of reusable bottles are using them regularly this year.
All people who buy reusable bottles use them every day.
Reusable bottles eliminate the need for tap water.
Most people in the survey bought bottles last year.
The survey included only athletes.

distractorRationale.B: Saying all buyers use bottles every day overstates the data, which only said most carry one on at least four days; the supportable inference is that many use them regularly.
distractorRationale.C: Eliminating the need for tap water is an unsupported leap about substitution; the survey only licenses the modest inference that many buyers use bottles regularly.
distractorRationale.D: Whether most survey respondents bought last year is not stated; the data describe behavior among buyers, supporting only that many of them use bottles regularly.
distractorRationale.E: Nothing restricts the sample to athletes; that is fabricated detail, whereas the safe inference is that many recent buyers are using bottles regularly.

explanation: The fact that the majority of buyers report frequent use allows us to infer regular usage among many of them, without generalizing to all.

passageOrStimulus: A city argues that a new protected bike lane reduced traffic congestion because average car travel time on the street fell by 12% after the lane opened.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

choices.text: A major road nearby was under construction during the same period, causing many drivers to avoid the street for unrelated reasons.
More bicycles were seen on the street after the lane opened.
Some residents like the bike lane.
The lane was painted green.
Parking spaces were removed near the street.

distractorRationale.B: More bicycles is expected and consistent with the city's story, not a weakener; to undercut the congestion claim you need a separate cause for the travel-time drop, like nearby road construction.
distractorRationale.C: Residents liking the lane is irrelevant to whether the lane caused faster travel; the weakener supplies an alternative explanation, the construction diverting drivers.
distractorRationale.D: The lane's color has no bearing on traffic flow; only an alternative cause for the 12% drop, such as the nearby road work, weakens the argument.
distractorRationale.E: Removing parking spaces would tend to be neutral or even reduce traffic in line with the city's claim, not against it; the genuine weakener is the unrelated construction that drew drivers away.

explanation: Road work on another route provides an alternative explanation for the decrease in vehicle traffic.

passageOrStimulus: A museum says that offering a discounted late-night ticket will increase total revenue.
stem: Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate the claim?

choices.text: Whether the discount would attract enough additional visitors to offset the lower ticket price.
Whether the museum's late-night visitors tend to spend more at the gift shop than daytime visitors do.
Whether nearby attractions also offer discounted late-night admission.
Whether the museum has enough staff willing to work late-night shifts.
Whether late-night visitors are more satisfied with their experience than daytime visitors.

distractorRationale.B: Gift-shop spending is a secondary revenue stream, not the core test of the ticket plan; the decisive question is whether added volume offsets the lower ticket price.
distractorRationale.C: What nearby attractions charge does not settle this museum's own revenue math; the key issue is whether enough extra visitors come to cover the discount.
distractorRationale.D: Staffing willingness is a feasibility detail, not the revenue question; to evaluate the claim you must know whether added attendance offsets the price cut.
distractorRationale.E: Visitor satisfaction does not directly bear on total revenue; the central evaluation point is whether the discount draws enough additional visitors to compensate for the lower price.

explanation: The central issue is whether the additional customer volume offsets the drop in unit price.

passageOrStimulus: A farmer claims that switching to drip irrigation will reduce water use without lowering crop output.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim?

choices.text: Fields using drip irrigation on similar crops used less water and produced similar yields over the same season.
Drip irrigation systems have become considerably cheaper to install in recent years.
Fields using drip irrigation on similar crops used far less water but produced noticeably smaller yields.
The farmer's region has experienced below-average rainfall for several seasons.
Drip irrigation delivers water directly to the roots of each plant.

distractorRationale.B: A cheaper install addresses cost, not the claim about water use and yield; the strengthener is comparable fields using less water with similar yields.
distractorRationale.C: Smaller yields actually weaken the claim that output stays level; support requires similar yields with less water, as in choice A.
distractorRationale.D: Low regional rainfall motivates saving water but does not show drip irrigation keeps yields up; the direct support is the comparable-field result.
distractorRationale.E: Explaining the mechanism does not prove the real-world outcome of equal yield with less water; the empirical comparison in choice A provides that support.

explanation: Comparable results with less water and similar yields directly support the claim.

passageOrStimulus: A restaurant says that adding a vegetarian tasting menu will attract new customers without losing existing ones.
stem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the claim depends?

choices.text: Current customers will not view the vegetarian menu as lowering the restaurant's overall quality.
The chef enjoys vegetables.
Vegetarian dishes are usually colorful.
The restaurant is open on Fridays.
Competitors also offer desserts.

distractorRationale.B: The chef's enjoyment of vegetables is irrelevant to retaining current customers; the needed assumption is that they will not see the new menu as lowering quality.
distractorRationale.C: Dish color is a cosmetic aside, not a precondition for keeping existing diners; the argument depends on current customers not perceiving a drop in quality.
distractorRationale.D: Friday hours are unrelated to whether the menu attracts new diners without losing old ones; the assumption is that current customers will not be put off.
distractorRationale.E: What competitors offer for dessert does not bear on this restaurant's customer retention; the load-bearing assumption is that current diners will not view the vegetarian menu as a quality decline.

explanation: It must be assumed that current customers will not be driven away by the new menu.

passageOrStimulus: A magazine concludes that readers prefer shorter articles because the average time spent on the magazine's website fell after article length was reduced.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the conclusion?

choices.text: The magazine also changed its homepage layout so that articles were more visible and easier to open quickly.
Some articles became shorter.
The website had fewer ads.
Readers complained less about navigation.
The magazine published more photos.

distractorRationale.B: Noting that some articles got shorter merely restates the premise and does not challenge the reader-preference conclusion; the weakener is the homepage redesign offering another cause for the time drop.
distractorRationale.C: Fewer ads does not clearly explain less time on site and could even be tangential; the real alternative cause is the layout change that let readers open articles faster.
distractorRationale.D: Fewer navigation complaints is a positive aside, not a competing explanation for reduced time spent; the weakener is the layout change making articles quicker to access.
distractorRationale.E: More photos does not account for shorter visits and might lengthen them; the conclusion is undercut by the homepage redesign that sped up article access.

explanation: The layout change provides an alternative explanation for the decrease in time spent on the site.

passageOrStimulus: A company reported that 70% of its remote employees live within commuting distance of an office, and 20% visit an office at least once a week.
stem: Which of the following can be inferred?

choices.text: Some remote employees who live near an office do not visit it weekly.
All remote employees live near an office.
Most remote employees visit an office weekly.
No remote employees work from home full time.
The company has only one office.

distractorRationale.B: Claiming all employees live near an office overstates the 70% figure; the valid inference is that some who live nearby do not visit weekly.
distractorRationale.C: Most visiting weekly contradicts the 20% figure; since 70% live near but only 20% visit, the inference is that some nearby residents do not visit each week.
distractorRationale.D: Nothing rules out full-time home workers; in fact up to 80% may never come in weekly, so the supported inference is that some nearby employees skip weekly visits.
distractorRationale.E: The number of offices is not stated; the percentages only support that some employees living within commuting distance do not visit an office weekly.

explanation: If 70% live within commuting distance and only 20% visit each week, some nearby residents do not visit every week.

passageOrStimulus: A neighborhood council claims that planting more trees will lower summer electricity bills.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim?

choices.text: Homes on tree-lined streets used less air conditioning during hot months than similar homes on treeless streets.
Residents on tree-lined streets reported feeling cooler outdoors in summer.
The proposed trees are a fast-growing species that reaches full height within a few years.
Homes on tree-lined streets had higher heating bills during the winter months.
Mature trees can lower the air temperature in their immediate vicinity.

distractorRationale.B: Feeling cooler outdoors is about ambient comfort, not indoor electricity bills; the strengthener ties trees to lower air-conditioning use, as choice A does.
distractorRationale.C: Fast growth speaks to how soon benefits arrive, not whether trees cut bills at all; support comes from tree-lined homes using less air conditioning.
distractorRationale.D: Higher winter heating bills would offset or undercut the savings claim, not strengthen it; the supporting evidence is reduced summer cooling on tree-lined streets.
distractorRationale.E: A general statement that trees lower nearby air temperature is suggestive but not tied to electricity use; the direct strengthener is the observed drop in air-conditioning use on tree-lined streets.

explanation: Lower air conditioning usage on tree-lined streets supports the link between trees and reduced utility bills.

passageOrStimulus: A company claims that allowing employees to choose their own start times will improve productivity because people work best at different hours.
stem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the claim depends?

choices.text: Employees with flexible start times will still be able to coordinate efficiently with one another.
Most employees prefer coffee to tea.
Adding plants is far less expensive than other measures the office considered.
Managers dislike early meetings.
The company will replace all desks.

distractorRationale.B: A coffee-versus-tea preference is irrelevant to whether flexible start times raise productivity; the assumption is that coordination among colleagues is preserved.
distractorRationale.C: Cost of plants belongs to a different argument entirely and has nothing to do with start times; the needed assumption is that flexibility will not harm team coordination.
distractorRationale.D: Managers disliking early meetings does not establish that flexible hours boost productivity; the argument assumes employees can still coordinate efficiently.
distractorRationale.E: Replacing all desks is unrelated to the scheduling claim; the load-bearing assumption is that staggered start times will not break down coordination.

explanation: It must be assumed that flexible scheduling will not harm coordination among colleagues.

passageOrStimulus: A school says its new tutoring program improved math scores because students who attended tutoring scored higher on the final exam.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the conclusion?

choices.text: Students who chose tutoring were already the strongest math students before the program began.
Students who attended tutoring also scored higher on the science final.
The school spent more on the tutoring program than it had planned.
Students who skipped tutoring reported studying less for the final exam.
The tutors were drawn from the school's most experienced teachers.

distractorRationale.B: Higher science scores too actually fits the self-selection worry and does not defend the math claim; the weakener is that tutored students were already the strongest, as choice A states.
distractorRationale.C: Spending more than planned concerns budget, not whether tutoring caused the score gain; the weakener is the pre-existing strength of the students who chose tutoring.
distractorRationale.D: Saying non-attendees studied less would, if anything, support the program rather than weaken it; the real weakener is that tutored students started out stronger.
distractorRationale.E: Experienced tutors would tend to strengthen the program's case, not undermine it; the conclusion is weakened by the fact that attendees were already top math students.

explanation: If enrolled students were already stronger to begin with, the score increase does not prove the program's effectiveness.

passageOrStimulus: A courier company is considering replacing diesel vans with electric vans.
stem: Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate the plan?

choices.text: Whether the electric vans can complete the typical daily route on a single charge.
Whether electric vans produce fewer emissions than the diesel vans they would replace.
Whether the company's drivers are willing to learn to operate electric vans.
Whether electric vans have become more popular among courier companies recently.
Whether the diesel vans could instead be retrofitted to run more efficiently.

distractorRationale.B: Comparing tailpipe emissions addresses environmental impact, not the operational feasibility the company must weigh; the key question is whether a daily route fits one charge.
distractorRationale.C: Driver willingness is a soft adoption issue, not the core feasibility test; what matters most is whether the vans can finish a typical route on a single charge.
distractorRationale.D: Popularity among other couriers is bandwagon evidence, not a test of this fleet's practicality; the decisive question is single-charge range versus daily routes.
distractorRationale.E: Retrofitting diesel vans is a different option, not an evaluation of the electric switch; the most useful question is whether electric vans cover the daily route per charge.

explanation: Feasibility depends largely on the actual range of electric vehicles.

passageOrStimulus: A report states that every branch of a chain adopted the same inventory software, but only some branches used it to automate reordering.
stem: Which of the following can be inferred?

choices.text: At least one branch used the software without automating reordering.
Most branches that adopted the software automated their reordering.
Every branch that automated reordering did so using the new software.
At least one branch failed to adopt the new inventory software.
Branches that automated reordering held less inventory than those that did not.

distractorRationale.B: Most automating reordering goes beyond what only-some-did supports; the firm inference is that at least one branch used the software without automation.
distractorRationale.C: That every automating branch used the new software is not stated and reverses the given direction; the supported inference is that at least one branch used it without automating.
distractorRationale.D: The premise says every branch adopted the software, so claiming one failed to adopt it contradicts the text; the valid inference is at least one used it without automating reordering.
distractorRationale.E: Inventory levels are never mentioned, so comparing them is unsupported; the only safe inference is that at least one branch used the software without automating reordering.

explanation: Since all branches adopted the software and only some automated their restocking, at least one branch used it without automation.

passageOrStimulus: An economist argues that a city's minimum-wage increase did not reduce employment because the number of jobs rose afterward.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

choices.text: The city's population and business activity both grew during the same period.
Some employers said the law was inconvenient.
Workers spent more money at local stores.
The increase was announced in the newspapers.
Other cities debated similar laws.

distractorRationale.B: Employers calling the law inconvenient is opinion and does not support the no-effect conclusion; the strengthener is concurrent population and business growth that explains the job rise.
distractorRationale.C: More local spending is a downstream effect, not evidence about employment causation; support comes from independent growth in population and business activity.
distractorRationale.D: Newspaper announcements are irrelevant to whether the wage hike cost jobs; the strengthener offers an alternative driver of job growth, namely a growing economy.
distractorRationale.E: Other cities debating laws says nothing about this city's employment; the argument is strengthened by the simultaneous population and business expansion.

explanation: Population growth and increased economic activity provide a plausible alternative explanation for the rise in employment.

passageOrStimulus: A gardener says that mulching the soil will reduce watering needs because it slows evaporation.
stem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the argument depends?

choices.text: The mulch will stay in place long enough to affect the soil's moisture.
Mulching will improve the garden's appearance as well as its moisture retention.
The mulch will not raise the cost of maintaining the garden.
Slowing evaporation is the only way to reduce how often the garden must be watered.
The plants in the garden require less water than they currently receive.

distractorRationale.B: Improving appearance is a side benefit, not something the water-saving argument needs; the required assumption is that mulch stays in place long enough to slow evaporation.
distractorRationale.C: Cost is outside the evaporation logic the argument relies on; the assumption is that the mulch remains in place to do its job.
distractorRationale.D: The argument never claims slowing evaporation is the only way to cut watering, so this is too strong to be a needed assumption; what it does assume is that the mulch stays put.
distractorRationale.E: Whether plants are currently overwatered is a separate matter; the assumption the argument depends on is that the mulch will remain long enough to affect soil moisture.

explanation: The argument assumes that the mulch remains in place long enough to fulfill its intended purpose.

passageOrStimulus: A hospital claims that a new triage system reduced wait times in the emergency department because the average wait dropped from 54 minutes to 41 minutes.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the claim?

choices.text: During the same period, many low-severity patients were diverted to an urgent-care clinic next door.
Nurses received training on the new system.
The emergency department renovated its entrance.
The hospital's parking lot was repaved.
Patients liked the new signage.

distractorRationale.B: Nurse training is part of implementing the new system, so it supports rather than weakens the claim; the weakener is the diversion of low-severity patients next door.
distractorRationale.C: Renovating the entrance does not plausibly explain a drop in average wait time; the genuine alternative cause is that minor cases were sent to the urgent-care clinic.
distractorRationale.D: A repaved parking lot has no bearing on emergency wait times; the weakener offers a competing cause, the diversion of low-severity patients.
distractorRationale.E: Patients liking the signage is irrelevant to wait duration; the claim is weakened because diverting minor cases could account for the shorter average wait.

explanation: Diverting minor cases can explain the reduction in wait times without attributing the entire improvement to the new triage process.

passageOrStimulus: A retailer claims that placing products near the checkout line increases impulse purchases.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim?

choices.text: Items moved to the checkout line sold more frequently than similar items kept elsewhere in the store.
Total store sales were higher in months when more products were displayed near the checkout.
Customers who waited longest in the checkout line bought the most items overall.
Items placed near the checkout were also featured in the store's weekly advertisements.
Most shoppers say they enjoy browsing the displays near the checkout line.

distractorRationale.B: Higher total store sales could stem from many factors and does not isolate the checkout effect; the strengthener compares the same items selling more when moved to the checkout.
distractorRationale.C: Longest-waiting customers buying most conflates dwell time with placement and is a confound, not support; the clean strengthener is items selling more after being moved to the checkout.
distractorRationale.D: If checkout items were also advertised, sales could be driven by the ads rather than placement, weakening the isolation; support requires the placement effect alone, as in choice A.
distractorRationale.E: Shoppers saying they enjoy the displays is attitude, not purchasing evidence; the claim is strengthened by items selling more frequently once placed at the checkout.

explanation: More frequent sales after repositioning near the checkout directly support the impulse-buying thesis.

passageOrStimulus: A publisher is deciding whether to print a novel in hardcover only or in both hardcover and paperback.
stem: Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate the decision?

choices.text: Whether the expected paperback demand is high enough to justify the additional printing and distribution costs.
Whether the hardcover edition is expected to sell well on its own.
Whether readers generally prefer paperbacks to hardcovers for novels of this kind.
Whether the paperback would be priced lower than the hardcover.
Whether the publisher's last novel sold more copies in paperback than in hardcover.

distractorRationale.B: How well the hardcover sells on its own does not address whether adding paperback is worthwhile; the decisive question is whether paperback demand justifies its extra costs.
distractorRationale.C: A general reader preference for paperbacks does not weigh demand against the added costs; the useful question is whether expected paperback demand covers the printing and distribution expense.
distractorRationale.D: That the paperback would be priced lower is a given of the format, not the cost-benefit test; the key question is whether its demand justifies the additional outlay.
distractorRationale.E: The last novel's split is weak past evidence for this title; the most useful question is whether this book's expected paperback demand justifies the added costs.

explanation: The critical point is whether pocket demand justifies the additional costs.

passageOrStimulus: A city survey showed that among residents who commute by train, most also use a bicycle at least once a week.
stem: Which of the following can be inferred?

choices.text: A majority of train commuters use bicycles weekly.
Most residents who use a bicycle weekly also commute by train.
Train commuters use bicycles more often than non-commuters do.
Most train commuters use a bicycle as their primary means of travel.
A majority of the city's residents use a bicycle at least once a week.

distractorRationale.B: Reversing the groups, most weekly cyclists are train commuters is not supported by a statement about train commuters; the valid inference is that most train commuters cycle weekly.
distractorRationale.C: Comparing commuters to non-commuters needs data on non-commuters, which is absent; the supported inference is simply that a majority of train commuters cycle weekly.
distractorRationale.D: Weekly use does not mean a bicycle is the primary mode of travel; the inference is only that most train commuters use a bicycle at least once a week.
distractorRationale.E: Generalizing to all residents goes beyond the train-commuter subgroup described; the safe inference is that a majority of train commuters cycle weekly.

explanation: The statement specifically concerns train commuters; it can be inferred that a majority of them also cycle each week.

passageOrStimulus: A manager argues that shortening the quarterly review meeting will not hurt planning because the team already discusses most issues by email.
stem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the argument depends?

choices.text: The issues discussed by email are sufficient to cover the topics that would otherwise require meeting time.
The manager enjoys short meetings.
Employees read every email immediately.
The team meets in a large conference room.
The review meeting always starts on time.

distractorRationale.B: The manager's enjoyment of short meetings is irrelevant to whether planning is preserved; the assumption is that email already covers the topics the meeting would.
distractorRationale.C: Immediate email reading is a stronger condition than the argument needs; what it assumes is that the email discussions adequately cover the necessary topics.
distractorRationale.D: Room size has nothing to do with whether shortening the meeting hurts planning; the assumption is that email handles the topics that would need meeting time.
distractorRationale.E: Punctual start times do not bear on whether email substitutes for discussion; the load-bearing assumption is that email already covers what the meeting otherwise would.

explanation: It must be assumed that email exchanges actually cover the topics that would have required more time in meetings.

passageOrStimulus: A café owner says that introducing self-service kiosks improved customer satisfaction because the average rating increased after the kiosks were installed.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

choices.text: The café simultaneously shortened its menu and removed several unpopular items.
The kiosks had touch screens.
Some customers used the kiosks more than once.
The café added more tables.
Ratings were collected online.

distractorRationale.B: Touch screens are a feature description, not a competing cause for higher ratings; the weakener is the simultaneous menu change offering an alternative explanation.
distractorRationale.C: Repeat kiosk use does not explain the rating rise and does not separate kiosks from other changes; the weakener is the simplified menu introduced at the same time.
distractorRationale.D: Adding tables could raise satisfaction, but the simplified menu is the change the explanation flags as the alternative cause; treat tables as a weaker near-miss and pick the menu confound.
distractorRationale.E: How ratings were collected does not provide a rival cause for the increase; the argument is weakened because the shortened menu may have driven the higher ratings.

explanation: The improvement in ratings may be due to the simplified menu rather than the kiosks themselves.

passageOrStimulus: A nutritionist claims that people who eat breakfast daily are less likely to snack late at night.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim?

choices.text: In a study, participants who ate breakfast every day reported fewer late-night snacks than similar participants who skipped breakfast.
People who eat breakfast daily tend to be more health-conscious in general.
People who snack late at night often report feeling tired the next morning.
Eating a larger breakfast leaves people feeling fuller throughout the morning.
Among people who already snack at night, those who add breakfast snack just as often.

distractorRationale.B: General health-consciousness is a confound that could explain less snacking by itself, not support for breakfast as the cause; the strengthener is the controlled study comparing similar participants.
distractorRationale.C: Late snackers feeling tired addresses an effect of snacking, not whether breakfast reduces it; support comes from the breakfast-versus-skipped comparison in choice A.
distractorRationale.D: A larger breakfast leaving people full speaks to one breakfast detail, not the daily-breakfast versus late-snacking link; the direct strengthener is the comparative study.
distractorRationale.E: If adding breakfast leaves snacking unchanged, that undercuts the claim rather than strengthening it; the support is fewer late-night snacks among daily breakfast eaters.

explanation: A comparative study showing less snacking among people who eat breakfast directly strengthens the conclusion.

passageOrStimulus: A delivery company is deciding whether to shift from same-day to next-day shipping for most orders.
stem: Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate the decision?

choices.text: Whether customers would cancel orders if delivery took one extra day.
Whether next-day shipping would lower the company's delivery costs.
Whether competitors offer same-day shipping on most orders.
Whether the company can deliver next-day orders on schedule.
Whether most current orders are already delivered the next day in practice.

distractorRationale.B: Lower delivery costs is a benefit of the switch but not the central risk; the decisive question is whether customers would cancel over the extra day.
distractorRationale.C: What competitors offer is context, not a test of how this company's customers react; the most useful question is whether the one-day delay would prompt cancellations.
distractorRationale.D: On-time delivery is an operational matter, while the key risk is customer reaction to slower shipping; evaluate whether customers would cancel given the extra day.
distractorRationale.E: If orders already arrive next day, the change may be moot, but the question that decides the plan is how customers respond to a one-day delay.

explanation: The essential issue is how customers will react to an additional one-day delay.

passageOrStimulus: A student says that studying in the library is better than studying at home because the library is quieter.
stem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the argument depends?

choices.text: The student can actually focus better in quiet places.
The library is open every day.
Books are available at the library.
The student owns a laptop.
Home internet is slow.

distractorRationale.B: Daily library hours are a logistics detail, not what the quieter-is-better claim rests on; the assumption is that quiet actually improves this student's focus.
distractorRationale.C: Book availability is irrelevant to the comparison about noise; the argument assumes that a quieter setting helps the student concentrate.
distractorRationale.D: Owning a laptop is not required for the quiet-based claim; the load-bearing assumption is that quiet surroundings improve the student's concentration.
distractorRationale.E: Slow home internet is a separate disadvantage not invoked by the quietness argument; the assumption is that the student focuses better in quiet places.

explanation: The argument must assume that quiet surroundings actually improve this student's concentration.

passageOrStimulus: A report says that all of a firm's interns received training, but only half received mentors.
stem: Which of the following can be inferred?

choices.text: At least one intern received training without a mentor.
No intern received both training and a mentor.
All interns had mentors.
Some interns were not trained.
The firm hired only one intern.

distractorRationale.B: No intern getting both contradicts the data, since all were trained and half also had mentors; the inference is that at least one was trained without a mentor.
distractorRationale.C: All having mentors contradicts the stated half; the supported inference is that at least one trained intern lacked a mentor.
distractorRationale.D: Some not trained contradicts the premise that all received training; the valid inference is that at least one trained intern had no mentor.
distractorRationale.E: A single intern is not implied and conflicts with half having mentors; the inference is that at least one intern was trained without a mentor.

explanation: Since all received training and only some have a mentor, at least one trainee received training without a mentor.

passageOrStimulus: A manufacturer claims that changing the packaging of its cereal increased sales because sales rose after the redesign.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the claim?

choices.text: The cereal was also placed on a lower shelf where shoppers were more likely to notice it.
The new package used brighter colors.
Some customers said they liked the old logo.
The redesign took six months.
The cereal was sold in several stores.

distractorRationale.B: Brighter colors are just a feature of the redesign, not an independent cause of higher sales; the weakener is the move to a more noticeable lower shelf.
distractorRationale.C: Some customers liking the old logo would, if anything, argue against the redesign helping; the weakener provides a rival cause, the better shelf placement.
distractorRationale.D: How long the redesign took does not explain why sales rose; the alternative cause is the cereal being moved to a more visible shelf.
distractorRationale.E: Being sold in several stores is neutral background, not a competing explanation; the claim is weakened because the lower, more visible shelf could have driven the sales increase.

explanation: The shelf repositioning provides a competing explanation for the increase in sales.

passageOrStimulus: A school district argues that later start times will improve attendance because more students arrive late on early-start days.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

choices.text: On days when classes started later, the district recorded fewer tardy arrivals than on comparable early-start days.
On days when classes started later, students reported getting more sleep the night before.
On days when classes started later, fewer students were absent for the entire day.
Neighboring districts that adopted later start times saw their test scores improve.
Most parents in the district favor moving to a later start time.

distractorRationale.B: More reported sleep is a plausible mechanism but not direct evidence of better attendance; the strengthener is the recorded drop in tardy arrivals on later-start days.
distractorRationale.C: Fewer full-day absences concerns absenteeism, while the argument is about late arrivals; the direct support is fewer tardies on later-start days.
distractorRationale.D: Improved test scores in other districts is off-topic and indirect; the strengthener is this district's own comparison showing fewer tardy arrivals with later starts.
distractorRationale.E: Parental approval is a preference, not evidence about attendance; the conclusion is supported by the observed reduction in late arrivals on later-start days.

explanation: A direct comparison showing fewer delays with a later start time supports the conclusion.

passageOrStimulus: A nonprofit is considering a fundraising dinner.
stem: Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate the plan?

choices.text: Whether the expected ticket sales would cover the cost of the venue and catering.
Whether the dinner would attract more attendees than last year's event.
Whether donors generally respond better to dinners than to mailed appeals.
Whether the nonprofit can secure a discounted rate on the venue.
Whether the dinner would raise the nonprofit's profile in the community.

distractorRationale.B: Beating last year's turnout is about attendance, not whether the dinner is financially viable; the decisive question is whether ticket sales cover venue and catering costs.
distractorRationale.C: A general preference for dinners over mailings does not settle this event's economics; the useful question is whether projected ticket revenue covers the costs.
distractorRationale.D: Securing a discounted venue helps one cost line but does not test overall viability; what matters is whether expected ticket sales cover the full cost of the event.
distractorRationale.E: Raising the nonprofit's profile is a soft benefit, not the financial test; the most useful question is whether ticket sales would cover venue and catering.

explanation: The decisive criterion is that the projected revenue covers the cost of the event.

passageOrStimulus: An app developer claims that adding a dark mode will reduce eye strain because users spend many hours on the app at night.
stem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the argument depends?

choices.text: The app is bright enough that reducing screen brightness would matter to users.
Most users live in apartments.
The app already has a search feature.
Users prefer free apps.
The developer can update the app quickly.

distractorRationale.B: Where users live is irrelevant to whether dark mode reduces eye strain; the assumption is that the current screen is bright enough for the change to matter.
distractorRationale.C: An existing search feature has nothing to do with eye strain from brightness; the argument assumes the app is bright enough for dark mode to have an effect.
distractorRationale.D: A preference for free apps does not bear on the eye-strain claim; the assumption is that reducing the app's brightness would actually matter to users.
distractorRationale.E: How fast the developer can update is a delivery issue, not the eye-strain logic; the load-bearing assumption is that the screen is currently bright enough for dark mode to help.

explanation: The argument assumes that the current screen is bright enough for dark mode to have a real effect on eye strain.

passageOrStimulus: A company's annual report says that revenue rose in every quarter, while expenses rose only in the first two quarters.
stem: Which of the following can be inferred?

choices.text: There was at least one quarter in which profit improved relative to the previous quarter.
Expenses never rose.
Revenue always exceeded expenses by the same amount.
The company had no debt.
Revenue grew more slowly than expenses in all quarters.

distractorRationale.B: Saying expenses never rose contradicts the premise that they rose in the first two quarters; the valid inference is that profit improved in at least one quarter.
distractorRationale.C: A constant revenue-minus-expense gap is far more specific than the data allow; the inference is that there was at least one quarter of improved profit.
distractorRationale.D: Nothing is stated about debt, so that cannot be inferred; the supported inference is that profitability improved in at least one quarter.
distractorRationale.E: Revenue growing more slowly than expenses in all quarters contradicts expenses being flat after quarter two; the inference is that profit improved in at least one quarter.

explanation: If revenue increased every quarter and expenses only rose at the start, there is at least one quarter in which profitability improved.

passageOrStimulus: An insurer says that its new phone-based screening system reduced fraudulent claims because the share of suspicious claims declined after it was introduced.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the claim?

choices.text: The system was introduced at the same time a new law imposed heavier penalties for fraud.
The total number of claims the insurer received rose after the system was introduced.
The screening system was more expensive to operate than the insurer had expected.
Most policyholders said the phone-based screening was easy to complete.
A rival insurer adopted a similar screening system the following year.

distractorRationale.B: A rise in total claims does not explain why the suspicious share fell and does not undercut the system's role; the weakener is the new law's heavier fraud penalties introduced at the same time.
distractorRationale.C: Higher operating cost concerns budget, not whether the system reduced fraud; the weakener offers an alternative cause, the simultaneous stricter penalties.
distractorRationale.D: Policyholders finding the screening easy is a positive aside, not a rival explanation; the claim is weakened by the concurrent law imposing tougher penalties.
distractorRationale.E: A rival adopting a similar system later says nothing about this insurer's decline; the weakener is the new law deterring fraud at the same time the system launched.

explanation: A new law with stricter penalties provides another plausible reason for the decline in suspicious requests.
